item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … Hmm… does working people to death count as natural causes? 'cause an anomaly that requires the Foundation to create paperwork gulags is very high in both hilarity and horror.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Sorry, I'm going to veto these edits. I think the link to temporal SCPs is tenuous (you could argue they might help in containment, but you could argue that about a wide variety of other effects, such as whatever random SCP that gave you more energy or concentration). The end-of-the-world scenario I'm vetoing as you wouldn't link XK-class scenarios or CK-class articles to one another just because of the scenario, though I grant NK is a bit less common.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: vetoed by author

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

Firstly, considering that this is a Keter-class anomaly that's really dangerous, I'd say that the Foundation might use some temporal anomalies to help speed the process of containment up. Some anomalies that come to mind are SCP-112 and SCP-2400.

Secondly, this mentions an "NK-class end-of-the-world scenario", which was used previously in SCP-622. I'd recommend a crosslink for that reason.

Editing this:

All individuals under the effect of an SCP-3925 event, once identified by local Foundation sources, are to be transported to the nearest Subdivision 03 facility. With the cooperation of national and international governments, these have been provided with the cover of government agencies relevant to the region. Affected individuals will be given an appropriate briefing regarding the agency needing to collect information from them, and reside on site while they complete SCP-3925-1 with the assistance of Foundation clerical staff. Once SCP-3925-1 has been completed to a satisfactory degree and the SCP-3925 event affecting an individual has concluded, they may then be released to their previous activities, with a subsequent 60-day observation period in the community.

To this:

All individuals under the effect of an SCP-3925 event, once identified by local Foundation sources, are to be transported to the nearest Subdivision 03 facility. With the cooperation of national and international governments, these have been provided with the cover of government agencies relevant to the region. Affected individuals will be given an appropriate briefing regarding the agency needing to collect information from them, and reside on site while they complete SCP-3925-1 with the assistance of Foundation clerical staff. Usage of SCP-112 and SCP-2400 is authorized to assist subjects with the completion of SCP-3925. Once SCP-3925-1 has been completed to a satisfactory degree and the SCP-3925 event affecting an individual has concluded, they may then be released to their previous activities, with a subsequent 60-day observation period in the community.

Editing this:

This would result in an eventual NK-class end-of-the-world scenario, with the mass of SCP-3925-1 predicted to exceed the mass of the Earth by the 350th iteration.

To this:

This would result in an eventual NK-class end-of-the-world scenario1, with the mass of SCP-3925-1 predicted to exceed the mass of the Earth by the 350th iteration.

Footnotes
1. For more information on NK-class "Grey Goop" end-of-the-world scenarios, personnel with appropriate clearance may consult the documentation for SCP-622.

item.title: Personal data
item.content:encoded: Based on this SCP, I can say with certainty that Mark SUCCerburg works for the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh, that's embarrassing, I thought I did a search for the titles.

I prefer not to repeat a title, so I've changed it to the B-plan ('It's All Papers and Forms')

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's gonna cause a lot of headaches for bots.

A lot of stuff for the IRC functions is driven partially by title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … Should repeat titles it be a problem? I mean, do we as a general practice ever call SCPs by their titles instead of their numbers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was going to bring this up as well. This is a great SCP but are repeat titles OK?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just fyi, there's already a scip with the title "Papers, Please": SCP-1526. It's not a problem, I just felt it warranted mentioning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is like a pop quiz… but from HELL!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one a lot! Only gripe is, from a technical standpoint, I'm not sure Foundation emails would be based on a domain like scp.org

They'd likely use something esoteric or nonpublic

That's just me though, and can easily be glossed over without issue.

Nice work! I'm working on a beauracratic nightmare jole article, this one is way absurd but creepy too.

"Do you denounce your god?"

Ahaha +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love how this skip takes a relatively simple concept, and runs with it. This is like how Extra Credits talked about maximum depth with minimum complexity. The anomaly itself is relatively simple, but the way this skip takes it is sublime.

My only real complaint is that the scope of this thing is massive. Even just dealing with the anomaly itself takes a lot of resources. How do you deal with people talking about it with other people?

Other than that, this is a solid skip.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple concept, extraordinary depth. Fine work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a whimsical article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any obtuse, confusing language is because I don't edit my writing nearly enough intentional as part of the theme of the SCP. I might have a look at it again.

I don't think the number of people should be blacked out as it specifically refers to the number of level 0 (ie. janitors and receptionists) which is hardly secret information. 40000 is about half the size of the IRS - while that's obviously still a huge organisation, unlike the IRS this is international. I think whether you find this ridiculous or not would be subjective based on your personal headcanon about how big the Foundation is.

However, I realised after writing the article that it's pretty over the top given that I specified the number of average events, so I'm going to change some of the numbers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the overall concept, but parts of the article read a little clunky to me, especially at the beginning. Also, I'd rather the amount of people related to this project be blacked out, instead of 40,000. That's an awful lot, and while the foundation obviously has extensive financial resources and manpower, I tend to dislike when containment procedures draw too much attention to this. I ended up neutral voting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't realize administrate also meant "to administer." I thought it was just "to govern, as an institution." You did me a learn!

Nice skip, by the way. So simple, so dangerous, and so doggone tedious to keep in check. Good stuff!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't see the typo, thanks for catching it.

Administrated is an appropriate word. Some sources argue that administer should be used for style reasons, though, so I changed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
End of third paragraph, administrated should be administered. Also, change faciitate to facilitate.
Just trying to help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the kind comments.

Reverted 'misspelling' edit; as far as I know, all words initially used are acceptable (eg. wellbeing vs well-being) and there is no requirement to use American spellings on this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just fill out these forms to prevent the Bureaucalypse. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I definitely did not expect to read about an end-of-the-world scenario in an SCP article about form letters.

This is very clever!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3925 Event Caught On Footage
nicememe

My legit thoughts when reading this. In a funny way.
I absolutely enjoy articles that have depth and implication.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. I always like skips that take a fairly simple concept but dive deep into the implications such a concept would have, and that's done quite well here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I said this on your other skip posted recently, but it bears repeating, the subtleties of this skip are what sells it so hard for me.

The anomaly itself, much like your steel ingot is kinda…eh. Eh as in not bad, just relatively benign. But there's a hell of a lot of implication and depth, for such a relatively simple concept.

+1.
